pi_items.source: Standard Bridge Command scenario file format (verified via simulator documentation)

pi_items.verified_value: IMO Resolution A.535(13) - adopted 17 November 1983, Recommendation on Emergency Towing Requirements for Tankers
Messenger line is a component of emergency towing arrangement (messenger lines are used to lift towing assembly and connect towing wire to disabled vessel)
Towing pennant is the wire rope towed between vessels in emergency towing arrangements, connected via fairlead and chafing chain
VHF (Very High Frequency) marine radio used for emergency maritime communication, with Channel 16 as international distress frequency and Channel 67 for ship-to-ship messages
Lee side is the downwind side of a vessel where the tug positions itself for safer approach and line connection during emergency towing
Catenary is the downward sag curve in a towline between two vessels that acts as a shock absorber, easing strain during towing and dampening jerks in rough weather
SECURITE (pronounced sea-cur-i-tay) is the maritime VHF radio safety signal used to transmit information about navigation safety and important weather warnings
Drift is unintended vessel movement caused by external forces (wind or current), critical consideration for disabled vessels in emergency towing scenarios

pi_items.source: Dover Strait geographic bounds (South Foreland area at ~51.13N, Strait center at ~51.02N, extending to ~50.9N in southern portion)
Dover Strait geographic bounds (encompasses Dover coast at 51.1333N and Strait area)
Dover Strait Traffic Separation Scheme boundaries (westerly limit of UK Inshore Traffic Zone)
Dover Strait geographic bounds (Strait center at ~1.47E, extending to ~1.6E easterly)
Bridge Command scenario specification (specific scenario not independently verified)

pi_items.verified_value: Beaufort Force 5: 17-21 knots, fresh breeze; Beaufort Force 6: 22-27 knots, strong breeze
Channel 16 = International distress/emergency frequency (all vessels monitor); Channel 67 = Commercial ship-to-ship working channel
Mid-morning hours appropriate for vessel navigation and training scenarios
Winter months have rougher sea conditions and lower visibility, appropriate for emergency towing exercise difficulty
5.0 nautical miles is standard maritime visibility threshold for navigation
Position falls within Dover Strait Traffic Separation Scheme SW lane boundaries

pi_items.metadata_value: Disabled vessel heading consistent with beam-on drift relative to wind
pi_items.verified_value: Drifting vessel heading approximately perpendicular (90°) to wind direction is correct maritime physics

privileged_info_summary: Emergency towing exercise task set in Dover Strait (50.95-51.15N, 1.10-1.60E) with realistic maritime concepts. All keywords verified as legitimate emergency towing procedure elements: messenger line and towing pennant are components of emergency towing arrangements per IMO A.535(13) (adopted 1983); VHF Channels 16/67 are correct for maritime emergency communication; lee side is proper towing approach position; catenary is correct rope configuration physics; SECURITE is valid maritime safety signal; drift is accurate vessel movement physics. Dover Strait TSS coordinates (51.05N, 1.35E) verified as within traffic separation scheme. Beaufort Wind Force 5-6 correctly specified as 17-27 knots. Disabled vessel beam-to-wind drift behavior is correct maritime physics. Task is synthetic simulator scenario with real geographic and procedural foundations.
